tweets.text: RT @JacobRobinsonJD: Should code ever lose First Amendment protections? This podcast covers free speech and software, including cryptography, cryptocurrency and AI. It should be the internet's most comprehensive. You'll hear from experts like @valkenburgh, @prestonjbyrne, @LazPieper, Sam Enzer, and Professors Eugene Volokh (@VolokhC) and @JaneYakowitz. We trace the history of Cold War export controls through to the recent restrictions on Anthropic's AI models, and explain why the Supreme Court has never definitively answered the question above. Timestamps: 0:00 Anthropic AI export controls 6:17 Intro 9:39 The First Amendment 10:38 @VolokhC on his free speech philosophy 13:42 Speech vs function 19:24 @valkenburgh interview 25:50 The Cold War, munitions list and Phil Zimmermann 29:57 The Bernstein case 34:53 Strict scrutiny 38:48 The Corley case 49:17 Sorrell, Stevens and where the Supreme Court is heading 53:25 @prestonjbyrne on the Supreme Court's AI case 57:23 Defense Distributed and 3D-printing 1:04:00 Where publication ends and conduct begins 1:15:20 Lowe v. SEC and the agency line 1:45:45 Compelled speech 2:04:17 @LazPieper on Sorrell and third-party liability 2:11:29 @prestonjbyrne on the GRANITE Act and foreign censorship Thank you to the presenting sponsor of this episode, @altitude. Nothing in this podcast is legal or investment advice.
tweets.summary: RT @JacobRobinsonJD: Should code ever lose First Amendment protections? This podcast covers free speech and software, including cryptography,…

tweets.text: Balaji is getting the Malaysia special. Malaysia Protest 4 Palestine isn’t the issue here. It’s Malaysian Pay 4 Play. You can’t do anything in the country without making sure you “align interests” with the right people. Lots of people clowning on Balaji but he’s not retarded. He definitely had some level of assurance before setting up in Forest City. It’s not hard to guess where his backers might be. Forest City is owned by 60% Chinese developers and 40% Johor, with the approval of the Sultan of Johor, the richest Malaysian Sultan, who is now literally King of Malaysia. The Chinese rugged Forest City after they withdrew from One Belt One Road. Forest City is now a ghost town - despite significant investment in - population is about 8000. About 1000 are Balaji’s people. A non negligible portion of the population service network school (the other small group of visitors are golfers). Without these USD wielding immigrants, many of the Forest City residents would have moved to Johor Bahru or another city. There are people in power in Malaysia that want to see Forest City revitalised. There’s a lot of money in that. Balaji’s 100m MYR investment isn’t much but it justifies them printing hundreds of millions more to continue development. He is in trouble now because he has found some success and now the other factions want their pound of flesh, knowing he has money. Malaysian authorities have this incredible superpower of being able to magic up licenses and regulations until they get paid. He’s currently being slapped by the education regulator and investigated by the immigration authorities. Note they haven’t kicked him out completely or shut the door to NS, it’s simply a lot of posturing. The message is clear - whoever was protecting you hasn’t paid us off. So get with the programme and *ahem* make sure you have the right licenses. I’d be very surprised if Balaji hasn’t already been solicited by a dozen middlemen offering to get him the right licenses to make the problems go away. Most of them likely scammers. Balaji’s difficulty level is also set at Indian, which is higher even than Chinese in Malaysia. The next few days are now critical for Balaji and will determine whether he will be heralded as the tech Prometheus of Malaysia, or persecuted as a Jew loving Indian that practises sodomy (a Malaysia special!) It all comes down to getting… the right licenses.
tweets.summary: Balaji is getting the Malaysia special. Malaysia Protest 4 Palestine isn’t the issue here. It’s Malaysian Pay 4 Play. You can’t do anything in the…

tweets.text: RT @punk5736: Ensuring that hyperscalers and leading AI labs contribute to security research in the digital commons is a necessary and just goal for the open-source community to pursue. The case for structured collaboration with open-source projects needs to be made unrelentingly, especially as the gap between publicly available and privately held digital intelligence widens. Infrastructure projects like the following would uniquely benefit because of their open architectures, inherent complexity, and substantive contributions: @linuxfoundation: The Linux kernel underpins cloud security, @Android and @GrapheneOS, millions of PCs, and most connected devices. @leanprover: The prover kernel ensures soundness and consistency across the Lean proof ecosystem, which is advancing mathematics and enabling computer scientists to confidently build more powerful abstractions. @ethereumfndn @ethereuminsti @ethlabs_org: Ethereum’s core protocol specifications and implementations provide a global commons for digital integrity, financial access, and coordination. RISC-V zero-knowledge virtual machines (@eth_proofs, @SuccinctLabs, @NexusLabs, @brevis_zk, @openvm_org): zkVM provers and verifiers are driving a new generation of software architectures that enable privacy and introduce a fairer topology to the internet. The privledged positions of @GoogleDeepMind @AnthropicAI @OpenAI @grok @Microsoft @facebook @amazon in advanced AI is rooted in open source and is sustained by a dynamic digital commons. It stands to reason that their current programs, where they exist, aren't nearly substantive enough. Meaningful token budgets should be allocated, and access to leading models should be provided to qualified sets of open-source projects.

tweets.text: Rep. French Hill received the 2026 Best of Bipartisan Collaboration Award yesterday alongside Georgia Democrat Lucy McBath. The award, presented by the Congressional Management Foundation, recognizes congressional offices for standout work reaching across the aisle to get things done for constituents. At the ceremony, Hill noted that “good policy attracts good partners.” His recent legislative efforts, from the housing bill to the CLARITY Act to the IGNITE HBCU Excellence Act, are prime examples of how that guiding philosophy draws people in from all sides of the political aisle. #arkansas #bipartisanship

tweets.text: RT @businessbarista: Kimi K3 owned the internet today. Read tons of content & observed 10 key patterns: 1) The open source-to-frontier gap went from a year+ behind to 6 months to 6 days, all within the last 12 months. 2) An open model debuted ahead of a flagship US model for the first time ever. Artificial Analysis scored K3 at 57. Opus 4.8 sits at ~56, GPT-5.6 Terra at 55. It's still behind Fable 5 and GPT 5.6 Sol. 3) K3 helped build itself. An early version of K3 did the majority of Moonshot's own kernel optimization work during development. One 15-hour unattended run made a core operation 2.5x faster. 4) It's cheap per token, not cheap per answer. Sticker price is 1/3 of Fable. But it only runs at max thinking effort and burns ~2x the tokens per response. @simonw measured 13,241 reasoning tokens to write a 3,417 token answer. 5) The era of dirt-cheap Chinese AI is ending. $3/$15 per million tokens. Hacker News called it "extremely high for a Chinese open-weight model." 6) Weights don't drop until July 27. Mentions of "open" quietly disappeared from the docs an hour after launch. 7) Even when the weights drop, you can't run them. 2.8 trillion parameters. Top Reddit joke: "2TB VRAM Is All You Need." Open weights increasingly means auditable by companies with GPU clusters, not runnable by you. 8) The "they just distill/copy" argument is dying in public. One of the most upvoted comments: you'd have to be "a complete ignorant or a complete bigot" to believe Chinese labs aren't legit at this point. 9) Day one user verdict: fast, but less accurate. "Faster than Claude, but less accurate. On par with GPT 5.5 perhaps, but not 5.6 or Fable." 10) The one thing everyone agrees on: competition is wonderful. Even the skeptics: "Say what you want about these Chinese models but they sure create competition and urgency in the space."
tweets.summary: RT @businessbarista: Kimi K3 owned the internet today. Read tons of content & observed 10 key patterns: 1) The open source-to-frontier gap went from…

tweets.text: As many know, I’ve been very critical of President Trump. In fact, I challenge anyone to find a Republican Nominee or Candidate for federal office who’s been as independent as I have. I literally said “F the Republican Party - F the Democrat Party” during a debate 🆚 @sethmoulton. Shortly after I announced I was running for U.S. Senate against @EdMarkey, I was interviewed by @kelleratlarge and I said, especially as someone who’s fought for regulatory clarity involving digital assets, including suing the @SECGov on behalf of retail investors, that I hated the Trump Meme coin (and all Meme coins for that matter), and that I absolutely hated the fact that the President and his family was getting heavily involved into Crypto - not just because of perceived conflicts of interests and appearances of impropriety but because it would make Crypto a partisan issue. Of course, according to @EricTrump, he and his family embraced Crypto because they were debanked by TradFi. We know Chokepoint 2.0 was real, so Eric’s claims can’t be easily dismissed. I knew the partisanship would happen because we have elected officials including @EdMarkey and @sethmoulton who simply: HATE DONALD TRUMP MORE THAN THEY LOVE AMERICA. @dmweisberger👇= 🎯 For 10 years in Crypto we’ve seen chaos, fraud, collapse, and bankruptcies. And finally, we have a chance to end the uncertainty and chaos. Why would Democrats prevent clarity for the rest of America’s investors, businesses and entrepreneurs? If your argument is because it might enrich Trump and his family, it’s been reported that Trump himself has made $1.4 billion dollars off crypto already. So, let me get this straight: You’re going to prevent America from securing the lead in the world related to financial innovation to prevent something that has already occurred? That makes perfect sense - in Washington. As Dave states: “The right approach is to propose a Bill that covers Crypto, Securities, [commodities], Derivatives & Prediction markets uniformly, for the executive AND legislative branches.” Yet, Congress still hasn’t ended insider trading related to the stock market. And they wonder why they have an approval rating of like 12%. My Washington Clean Hands Act will end all the grift. In the meantime, maybe put America’s interests first.

tweets.text: I'm genuinely confused by the argument here. It seems to be: open-weight AI models reduce the profits of American VC-backed companies; many leading open models currently come from China; therefore open AI is somehow "communist." We've never applied that logic to any other technology. Linux, Apache, PostgreSQL, and countless other open-source projects destroyed proprietary software margins and dramatically reduced returns for some investors. We called that competition and innovation—not communism. If the concern is reliance on Chinese AI, make that argument. If the concern is national security, make that argument. But "this technology makes it harder for incumbents to earn monopoly-like returns" has never been a definition of communism. It's often just what technological progress and real free markets look like.

tweets.text: Tomorrow marks one year since my GENIUS Act became law. Before GENIUS, America lacked a clear federal framework for stablecoins, a gap that threatened innovation, competitiveness, and the long-term strength of the U.S. dollar. One year later, the United States has the first comprehensive federal framework for payment stablecoins, positioning America not just to participate in the digital asset economy, but to lead it. It was a watershed moment, and it’s only the beginning.

tweets.text: Great discussion with the top execs from crypto’s top trade associations at @hlglobal_ yesterday. We covered the Clarity Act; everyone wants the bill done, and a handful of issues remain outstanding with time running short. My fellow panelists were still optimistic about passage before August recess…more optimistic than me. We also talked SEC/CFTC; both agencies are well informed and working hard on Project Crypto. No matter which way Clarity goes, they will each have a lot to do in the coming months and years. Thank you @lindsayfraser0 @_jikim @CodyCarboneDC for your hard work and taking the time during a busy week! 🙏

tweets.text: RT @austincampbell: With Clarity back in the spotlight, one particular topic generating angst is the BRCA. @valkenburgh has written about this. @davidzmorris has written about this. @amandatums and DeFi education fund have written about this. However, I want to make a specific point clear on why this is such a big issue after the Tornado Cash case and @rstormsf's conviction for unlicensed money transmission. First, the crime he was convicted of did not require intent. It was "you did a thing where you needed to have a license for X, and you did not have a license for X". It's that simple. Those sorts of crimes are a special class where it's exceptionally important that, if you are going to enforce them, the obligation be clear, understandable, and known beforehand. Second, Roman was NOT convicted of conspiracy crimes around money laundering and sanctions. The jury couldn't come to a decision on those. Those crimes require some amount of knowledge and/or intent, meaning if you genuinely did not know the funds were criminal, and weren't so grossly negligent that any half-assed idiot should have known, you can't be convicted. So what's the problem? Why do I think we need the BRCA? Why have I been on my own high horse in the newsletter about this repeatedly? The reason: because the government itself cannot fucking tell us where the line is for money transmission. One, I think of Roman's conviction stands at the district court, this is the best and strongest line of appeal, possibly all the way to the top in the current era. If the government itself cannot tell me where the line is, that's a problem. Don't believe me? FinCEN, the people who look at this, published guidance about control and how money transmission works that drew a clear line, because it's important that your web browser, your ISP, and your computer manufacturer are not money transmitters. They aren't and can't be licensed, they cannot meaningfully fulfill those obligations, and saying they have to amounts to simply banning things, including all kinds of speech. The DoJ, on the other hand, because the prosecutors were entirely indifferent to the secondary consequences of their actions, did the equivalent of bombing a bus full of schoolchildren to kill one bad guy by arguing that, you know, you actually don't need control, you just need some undisclosed and undefined combination of things that feel, to a boomer who doesn't understand technology, like money transmission happened. Which now literally means your web browser is probably a money transmitter, if you understand how this stuff works. That is not good. And the implications of that go far, far beyond crypto. One, for basic rule of law and fair notice about crimes, but two, because for the love of god, your web browser is NOT a money transmitter and you need a clear, defining line as to where that boundary is so people can stay on either side of it. Or, in the simplest terms, if the people who were responsible for giving you a license would have said "no you don't need one" and the DoJ prosecutes you anyways for not having a license, something is horribly, horribly broken.
